content:
# Genesis Chapter 15 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 8v-9r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_015_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_015_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 10-11 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0014

---

*The Word of the LORD to Abram: Shield and Reward (vv. 1–3)*

**15:1** After these things [אַחַר הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה] the word of the LORD [דְּבַר-יְהוָה] came to Abram [הָיָה אֶל-אַבְרָם] in a vision [בַּמַּחֲזֶה] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Fear not [אַל-תִּירָא] Abram [אַבְרָם] — I [am] your shield [אָנֹכִי מָגֵן לָךְ] your reward very great [שְׂכָרְךָ הַרְבֵּה מְאֹד].

**15:2** And Abram said [וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָם]: Lord GOD [אֲדֹנָי יְהוִה] what will you give me [מַה-תִּתֶּן-לִי] and I [am] going childless [וְאָנֹכִי הוֹלֵךְ עֲרִירִי] and the son of acquisition of my house [וּבֶן-מֶשֶׁק בֵּיתִי] he [is] this Eliezer of Damascus [הוּא דַּמֶּשֶׂק אֱלִיעֶזֶר]?

**15:3** And Abram said [וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָם]: Look [הֵן] — to me you have not given seed [לִי לֹא נָתַתָּה זָרַע] and look [וְהִנֵּה] — a son of my house [בֶּן-בֵּיתִי] will be my heir [יוֹרֵשׁ אֹתִי].

---

*The Star Promise (vv. 4–5)*

**15:4** And look [וְהִנֵּה] — the word of the LORD came to him [דְּבַר-יְהוָה אֵלָיו] saying [לֵאמֹר]: This one will not be your heir [לֹא יִירָשְׁךָ זֶה] — but rather [כִּי-אִם] the one who will come out from your own innards [אֲשֶׁר יֵצֵא מִמֵּעֶיךָ] — he will be your heir [הוּא יִירָשֶׁךָ].

**15:5** And he brought him outside [וַיּוֹצֵא אֹתוֹ הַחוּצָה] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Look now toward the heavens [הַבֶּט-נָא הַשָּׁמַיְמָה] and count the stars [וּסְפֹר הַכּוֹכָבִים] if you are able to count them [אִם-תּוּכַל לִסְפֹּר אֹתָם] and he said to him [וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ]: So shall be your seed [כֹּה יִהְיֶה זַרְעֶךָ].

---

*Faith Reckoned as Righteousness (v. 6)*

**15:6** And he believed [וְהֶאֱמִן] in the LORD [בַּיהוָה] and he reckoned it [וַיַּחְשְׁבֶהָ] to him [לוֹ] as righteousness [צְדָקָה].

---

*The Land Promise and Covenant Preparation (vv. 7–11)*

**15:7** And he said to him [וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו]: I [am] the LORD [אֲנִי יְהוָה] who brought you out [אֲשֶׁר הוֹצֵאתִיךָ] from Ur of the Chaldeans [מֵאוּר כַּשְׂדִּים] to give you [לָתֶת לְךָ] this land [אֶת-הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת] to inherit it [לְרִשְׁתָּהּ].

**15:8** And he said [וַיֹּאמַר]: Lord GOD [אֲדֹנָי יְהוִה] by what [בַּמֶּה] shall I know [אֵדַע] that I will inherit it [כִּי אִירָשֶׁנָּה]?

**15:9** And he said to him [וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו]: Take for me [קְחָה-לִי] a heifer threefold [עֶגְלָה מְשֻׁלֶּשֶׁת] and a she-goat threefold [וְעֵז מְשֻׁלֶּשֶׁת] and a ram threefold [וְאַיִל מְשֻׁלָּשׁ] and a turtledove [וְתֹר] and a young pigeon [וְגוֹזָל].

**15:10** And he took for him [וַיִּקַּח-לוֹ] all these [אֶת-כָּל-אֵלֶּה] and he divided them [וַיְבַתֵּר אֹתָם] in the middle [בַּתָּוֶךְ] and he placed [וַיִּתֵּן] each half toward its companion [אִישׁ-בִּתְרוֹ לִקְרַאת רֵעֵהוּ] but the birds [וְאֶת-הַצִּפֹּר] he did not divide [לֹא בָתָר].

**15:11** And the birds of prey [וַיֵּרֶד הָעַיִט] came down [עַל-הַפְּגָרִים] upon the carcasses [הַנְּפָלִים] and Abram drove them away [וַיַּשֵּׁב אֹתָם אַבְרָם].

---

*The Deep Sleep and the Prophecy of Egypt (vv. 12–16)*

**15:12** And it was [וַיְהִי] the sun was going [הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ לָבוֹא] to set [לָבוֹא] and a deep sleep [וְתַרְדֵּמָה] fell [נָפְלָה] upon Abram [עַל-אַבְרָם] and look [וְהִנֵּה] — a dread [אֵימָה] a great darkness [חֲשֵׁכָה גְדֹלָה] falling [נֹפֶלֶת] upon him [עָלָיו].

**15:13** And he said to Abram [וַיֹּאמֶר לְאַבְרָם]: Knowing you shall know [יָדֹעַ תֵּדַע] that sojourners [כִּי-גֵר] your seed will be [יִהְיֶה זַרְעֲךָ] in a land not theirs [בְּאֶרֶץ לֹא לָהֶם] and they will serve them [וַעֲבָדוּם] and they will afflict them [וְעִנּוּ אֹתָם] four hundred years [אַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה].

**15:14** And also [וְגַם] the nation [אֶת-הַגּוֹי] whom they serve [אֲשֶׁר יַעֲבֹדוּ] I will judge [דָּן אָנֹכִי] and after that [וְאַחֲרֵי-כֵן] they will come out [יֵצְאוּ] with great property [בִּרְכֻשׁ גָּדוֹל].

**15:15** And you [וְאַתָּה] — you will go [תָּבוֹא] to your fathers [אֶל-אֲבֹתֶיךָ] in peace [בְּשָׁלוֹם] you will be buried [תִּקָּבֵר] in a good old age [בְּשֵׂיבָה טוֹבָה].

**15:16** And the fourth generation [וְדוֹר רְבִיעִי] they will return here [יָשׁוּבוּ הֵנָּה] for the iniquity of the Amorites [כִּי לֹא-שָׁלֵם עֲוֹן הָאֱמֹרִי] is not complete [עַד-הֵנָּה] until now [עַד-הֵנָּה].

---

*The Theophany: Smoking Fire Pot and Flaming Torch (vv. 17–18a)*

**15:17** And it was [וַיְהִי] the sun had set [הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ בָּאָה] and there was thick darkness [וְעֲלָטָה הָיָה] and look [וְהִנֵּה] — a smoking fire pot [תַּנּוּר עָשָׁן] and a flaming torch [וְלַפִּיד אֵשׁ] that passed [אֲשֶׁר עָבַר] between these pieces [בֵּין הַגְּזָרִים הָאֵלֶּה].

**15:18** On that day [בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא] the LORD cut [כָּרַת יְהוָה] with Abram [אֶת-אַבְרָם] a covenant [בְּרִית] saying [לֵאמֹר]: To your seed I have given [לְזַרְעֲךָ נָתַתִּי] this land [אֶת-הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת] from the river of Egypt [מִנְּהַר מִצְרַיִם] to the great river [עַד-הַנָּהָר הַגָּדֹל] the river Euphrates [נְהַר-פְּרָת].

---

*The Ten Nations and the Land Boundaries (vv. 19–21)*

**15:19** The Kenite [אֶת-הַקֵּינִי] and the Kenizzite [וְאֶת-הַקְּנִזִּי] and the Kadmonite [וְאֶת-הַקַּדְמֹנִי].

**15:20** And the Hittite [וְאֶת-הַחִתִּי] and the Perizzite [וְאֶת-הַפְּרִזִּי] and the Rephaim [וְאֶת-הָרְפָאִים].

**15:21** And the Amorite [וְאֶת-הָאֱמֹרִי] and the Canaanite [וְאֶת-הַכְּנַעֲנִי] and the Girgashite [וְאֶת-הַגִּרְגָּשִׁי] and the Jebusite [וְאֶת-הַיְבוּסִי].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### On 15:1 — "After These Things": Narrative Continuity and the First Davar-YHWH Formula

**אַחַר הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה** — "After these things."

The phrase **אַחַר הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה** ("after these things/words") is the standard transitional formula for moving from one narrative unit to the next (compare 22:1; 22:20; 39:7; 40:1). Here it connects Genesis 15 directly and immediately to the events of Genesis 14: the war of kings, Abram's rescue of Lot, the encounter with Melchizedek, and — crucially — Abram's refusal of wealth from the king of Sodom. The vision of 15:1 appears to come precisely *after* Abram has sacrificed potential enrichment from the Sodomite king, and the opening divine words — "I am your shield, your reward very great" — answer that sacrifice directly. Abram gave up worldly reward; YHWH himself is his reward.

**דְּבַר-יְהוָה הָיָה אֶל-אַבְרָם** — "the word of the LORD came to Abram."

This is the **first occurrence of the davar-YHWH formula** in the entire Bible — the formula that will become the characteristic opening of prophetic revelation throughout the Former and Latter Prophets ("the word of the LORD came to...": cf. 1 Sam. 15:10; 2 Sam. 7:4; 1 Kings 6:11; Jer. 1:4; Ezek. 1:3; Hos. 1:1; Joel 1:1; Amos 3:1, and hundreds of other instances). Its first occurrence in the Abrahamic narrative marks Abram as, among other things, the prototype of the prophetic figure who receives divine address. The formula is not used in Genesis 1-14 in this exact form; its appearance here is inaugural.

**בַּמַּחֲזֶה** — "in a vision."

The Hebrew noun **מַחֲזֶה** (machazeh) derives from the root **חָזָה** (chazah), the standard verb for prophetic vision, which gives us **חֹזֶה** (choze, "seer") and **חֲזוֹן** (chazon, "vision") — the vocabulary of the prophetic experience throughout the Hebrew Bible. The use of **מַחֲזֶה** here (as opposed to **חֲלוֹם**, "dream") marks this as a visionary revelation rather than a dream — Abram is presumably awake and receiving an ecstatic prophetic vision (contrast 15:12, where the **תַּרְדֵּמָה** "deep sleep" falls at sunset; the vision of vv. 1-11 appears to occur before this sleep).

**אַל-תִּירָא אַבְרָם** — "Fear not, Abram."

The divine command **אַל-תִּירָא** ("fear not") is the most frequent reassurance formula in the Hebrew Bible. The question of what Abram fears is answered by context and by what follows: he has just completed a night raid against four kings, has enemies, has refused the Sodomite king's offer. But more deeply, the oracle responds to what v. 2-3 will reveal as Abram's inner anguish — he has no heir. The command addresses both external threat and internal anguish.

---

### On 15:1 — מָגֵן / שְׂכָרְךָ: The Shield and the Reward (Major Crux)

**אָנֹכִי מָגֵן לָךְ שְׂכָרְךָ הַרְבֵּה מְאֹד** — "I am your shield, your reward very great."

This clause presents one of the most debated syntactic ambiguities in the book of Genesis. The Hebrew reads:

> אָנֹכִי מָגֵן לָךְ שְׂכָרְךָ הַרְבֵּה מְאֹד

There are two grammatical structures possible:

**Reading 1 — Two parallel nominal clauses (the EMET rendering):**
> "I am your shield" / "your reward [is] very great"

On this reading, **אָנֹכִי מָגֵן לָךְ** ("I [am] your shield") is the first clause — YHWH himself is Abram's shield/protector. Then **שְׂכָרְךָ הַרְבֵּה מְאֹד** ("your reward [is] very great") is a second independent clause: Abram's reward will be exceedingly large. This yields two distinct divine assurances: protection and reward.

**Reading 2 — One clause with magen as a verbal noun / construct:**
> "I am your shield, your very great reward"

On this reading, **מָגֵן** functions predicatively as "one who shields," and **שְׂכָרְךָ** ("your reward") is in apposition to or in further predication with **אָנֹכִי**: "I am your protection, I myself am your very great reward." YHWH is not merely *giving* Abram a reward; YHWH *is* the reward. This reading has the deeper theological resonance: what Abram receives is not wealth or land per se, but the LORD himself.

**Reading 3 — Magen as a participle, sakan as alternative (a minority textual tradition):**
Some ancient interpreters (and the LXX in a few manuscript traditions) read **שְׂכָרְךָ** ("your reward") in light of an alternative root **שָׂכַן** (sakan) — "to place, set, establish" — though this reading is not supported by the Leningrad Codex. The MT reads clearly **שְׂכָרְךָ** (sakhar = "your reward/wages"). This variant has minimal manuscript support and the EMET does not adopt it.

**The LXX at 15:1:** Codex Vaticanus reads: **ἐγώ εἰμι ὑπερασπίζω σου· ὁ μισθός σου πολὺς ἔσται σφόδρα** — "I am shielding you; your reward will be very great." This confirms Reading 1: the LXX reads two clauses, not one. **ὑπερασπίζω** ("I am shielding/protecting," a Piel-type intensive) = **מָגֵן** as divine protection. **ὁ μισθός σου πολύς** = **שְׂכָרְךָ הַרְבֵּה**. The LXX does not support the reading "I am your very great reward" (Reading 2) but rather "I am shielding you; your reward will be very great" — the reward remaining distinct from YHWH himself.

**The EMET renders: "I [am] your shield / your reward very great"** — retaining the paratactic Hebrew structure, allowing both readings to resonate. The ambiguity between "YHWH is your shield and your reward will be great" and "YHWH himself is your very great reward" is a productive theological ambiguity that the Hebrew preserves and the translation should not resolve.

---

### On 15:2 — אֲדֹנָי יְהוִה: Lord GOD

**אֲדֹנָי יְהוִה** — "Lord GOD."

This is the first occurrence of the compound address **אֲדֹנָי יְהוִה** in the Pentateuch, a form that will become common in the prophetic literature (especially Ezekiel, where it appears over 200 times). The vocalization of the Tetragrammaton is modified to **יְהוִה** (with a patach under the he) when combined with **אֲדֹנָי** to avoid repeating the same vowels twice in oral reading (since **אֲדֹנָי** is read aloud wherever **יְהוָה** appears; the modified pointing distinguishes the combination). The EMET renders the combination as "Lord GOD" — with "Lord" representing **אֲדֹנָי** and "GOD" (small capitals) representing the Tetragrammaton, following the convention established for the EMET throughout — though the more common convention in most translations is to render **אֲדֹנָי יְהוִה** as "Lord GOD" (capital G, small capitals OD).

---

### On 15:2 — בֶּן-מֶשֶׁק בֵּיתִי הוּא דַּמֶּשֶׂק אֱלִיעֶזֶר: The Eliezer Syntax (Major Crux)

**וּבֶן-מֶשֶׁק בֵּיתִי הוּא דַּמֶּשֶׂק אֱלִיעֶזֶר** — "and the son of acquisition of my house — he [is] this Eliezer of Damascus."

This clause is one of the most syntactically difficult in all of Genesis. The text reads:

> וּבֶן-מֶשֶׁק בֵּיתִי הוּא דַּמֶּשֶׂק אֱלִיעֶזֶר

The difficulties are multiple and compound one another:

**1. מֶשֶׁק (meshek):** This word is a **hapax legomenon** — it appears only here in the entire Hebrew Bible. Its meaning must be reconstructed from context and cognates. Major proposals:
- **From מָשַׁק (mashak), "to draw out, lead":** thus **בֶּן-מֶשֶׁק** = "son of drawing/inheriting" = "one who draws/leads [the household]," i.e., the head servant or steward. The verb משׁך is used elsewhere for drawing or extending; a ben-meshek would be "a son of extension" = heir or steward-apparent.
- **From the Akkadian cognate mašqu, "drinking vessel, cup":** possibly connected with household management or stewardship. Speculative.
- **As "acquisition, possession":** "son of acquisition of my house" = the one who has been acquired as the inheritor, i.e., a slave who has been elevated to heir status. This would be the translation adopted by the EMET.

**2. הוּא דַּמֶּשֶׂק (hu' Dammesek, "he [is] Damascus"):** The word order is extremely unusual. The sentence appears to place the proper name before the personal name: "he [is] Dammesek Eliezer" — or perhaps "this Damascus [man], Eliezer." Why does the text name the city before the person? Several explanations:
- **Dammesek** may be functioning as a gentilicial adjective: "the Damascene Eliezer" = "Eliezer who is from Damascus." The word order places the geographic identification first for emphasis: *this* man is not from among Abram's own family or people but from Damascus.
- The syntax may reflect a *casus pendens* (dangling subject) construction: "the son of acquisition of my house — he: Damascus, Eliezer" = "the one who is heir to my house is this: a Damascene, Eliezer by name."
- The Syriac and Targum Onkelos both struggle with this phrase; Onkelos renders it somewhat paraphrastically. The LXX (Vaticanus) reads: **ὁ δὲ υἱὸς Μασεκ τῆς οἰκογενοῦς μου, οὗτος Δαμασκὸς Ἐλιέζερ** — "and the son of Masek of my house-born [servants], this [is] Damascus Eliezer." The LXX transliterates **מֶשֶׁק** as *Masek* (treating it as a proper name, perhaps a female slave Masek who bore Eliezer) — a fascinating interpretive move, but almost certainly secondary. The MT does not read **מֶשֶׁק** as a personal name; the construct chain **בֶּן-מֶשֶׁק בֵּיתִי** points to a common noun.

**The EMET renders as literally as possible:** "the son of acquisition of my house — he [is] this Eliezer of Damascus." The syntax is awkward in English because it is syntactically awkward in Hebrew; preserving that awkwardness is more honest than smoothing it. What is clear from the context is that Abram identifies a named individual — Eliezer, associated with Damascus — as the one who will inherit his estate in the absence of a biological son. This sets up the pathos of the exchange: despite divine promises, Abram has no heir, and his estate will pass to a servant.

---

### On 15:6 — וְהֶאֱמִן בַּיהוָה וַיַּחְשְׁבֶהָ לוֹ צְדָקָה: Faith and Righteousness (THE Crucial Verse)

**וְהֶאֱמִן בַּיהוָה וַיַּחְשְׁבֶהָ לוֹ צְדָקָה** — "And he believed in the LORD and he reckoned it to him as righteousness."

This single verse is, in the words of no hyperbole, one of the most theologically important sentences in the entire Bible. It is directly quoted in Romans 4:3, Galatians 3:6, and James 2:23, and it forms the scriptural foundation for Paul's entire theology of justification by faith. Every word requires careful attention.

**וְהֶאֱמִן (ve-he'emin) — "and he believed":**

The verb is the **Hiphil perfect of אָמַן (aman)** — the same root that gives us **אָמֵן** ("amen") and **אֱמוּנָה** (emunah, "faithfulness/faith"). The Hiphil of **אָמַן** means:
- "To trust, to have confidence in" (with the preposition ב, *be*): "to trust in" someone or something
- "To consider reliable, to hold as firm/steady" — in effect, "to lean one's weight upon"
- NOT merely intellectual assent but a relational, trust-posture orientation toward a person

The preposition **בַּיהוָה** ("in the LORD") — bet plus the Tetragrammaton — marks the direction of the trust: Abram's confidence is directed *toward* and *into* YHWH. He trusts the LORD's person, not merely his proposition. The timing is significant: Abram has just received the star-promise (v. 5) — an astronomically vast and humanly impossible promise — and his response is trust. He trusts YHWH to do what YHWH has promised even though Abram and Sarai are aged and childless.

The Hiphil form **הֶאֱמִן** carries a causative-declarative nuance: "he made [something/himself] firm" in YHWH. The LXX renders this as **ἐπίστευσεν Αβραμ τῷ θεῷ** — "Abram trusted/believed in God" — using the dative (τῷ θεῷ) rather than the preposition εἰς ("into"). The LXX's dative construction ("believed to/in God") was quoted verbatim by Paul in Romans 4:3 (ἐπίστευσεν δὲ Ἀβραὰμ τῷ θεῷ) from the LXX. The difference between the dative (LXX) and the prepositional εἰς construction represents a minor nuance but both point to personal trust directed toward a person.

**וַיַּחְשְׁבֶהָ (va-yachshevehah) — "and he reckoned it":**

The verb **חָשַׁב** (chashav) means "to think, reckon, count, calculate, consider, account." The subject of **וַיַּחְשְׁבֶהָ** is disputed — a matter of intense exegetical importance:

- **Subject = YHWH:** "And *he* [= the LORD] reckoned it to him as righteousness." On this reading, God is the one who performs the reckoning: God counted Abram's faith as righteousness. This is the reading of Paul (Rom. 4:3-5): the subject of the counting is God, who justifies the ungodly. It is the dominant reading in Christian exegesis.
- **Subject = Abram:** "And *he* [= Abram] reckoned it [= YHWH's word/promise] to him [= YHWH] as righteousness." On this reading, *Abram* counted YHWH's promise as righteous, reliable, trustworthy. This is the reading preferred by some Jewish interpreters and is grammatically possible since the preceding subject is Abram.

The pronoun **הָ** on **וַיַּחְשְׁבֶהָ** (the suffix "-ha," 3fs) — "he reckoned *it*" — refers to the preceding act of believing. But what exactly is the antecedent? It could be:
1. Abram's act of faith (emunah) — "he [YHWH] reckoned *it* [= the faith] to him [= Abram] as righteousness."
2. The promise/word of YHWH — "he [Abram] reckoned *it* [= the divine word] as righteousness [= reliable, just]."

The EMET's rendering — "and he reckoned it to him as righteousness" — preserves the ambiguity of the Hebrew exactly, neither naming YHWH nor Abram as subject. This is consistent with the EMET's policy of not resolving ambiguity the text does not resolve. The verb form and the pronouns are equally consistent with either subject. The text itself leaves the subject implicit.

**צְדָקָה (tsedaqah) — "righteousness":**

**צְדָקָה** is the feminine noun from the root **צָדַק** (tsadak) — "to be right, just, righteous, in the right." The noun **צְדָקָה** in its semantic range covers:
1. **Conformity to a standard** — being what one ought to be in a given relationship or context
2. **Right-standing in relationship** — being in proper covenant alignment with another party (God or human)
3. **Saving acts, acts of deliverance** — especially in later biblical poetry (parallel to **יְשׁוּעָה**, salvation, in Deutero-Isaiah)
4. **Almsgiving/charity** — in later Hebrew (this sense is not operative here)

In Genesis 15:6, **צְדָקָה** refers to a relational status — Abram's faith-stance toward YHWH is *counted as* constituting him in right-standing before God. This is the most precise technical meaning: not that faith is a meritorious act that *earns* righteousness, but that faith is the *mode* by which YHWH reckons (credits, accounts, imputes) Abram as being in right relationship. The accounting metaphor (**חָשַׁב**) is commercial/legal: it is a ledger-entry, a formal imputation.

**Paul's use (Romans 4):** Paul quotes Genesis 15:6 as the cornerstone of his argument that justification is *not* by works of law (since it precedes the law of circumcision, Gen. 17, and the Mosaic law) but by faith-trust in God's promise. The LXX's **ἐλογίσθη αὐτῷ εἰς δικαιοσύνην** ("it was reckoned/counted to him for/as righteousness") becomes Paul's key formula. Abram is justified before circumcision (Gen. 17), before the Mosaic law (Exod. 20), before any legal requirement — on the basis of trusting YHWH's promise alone.

**James's use (James 2:23):** James quotes the same verse to argue that faith without works is dead — but uses it differently: for James, the faith of 15:6 is demonstrated and confirmed by the obedience of the Akedah (Gen. 22), which "fulfilled" (ἐπληρώθη) the scripture of 15:6. Paul and James are not contradicting each other but using the verse to address different errors: Paul addresses those who think works earn standing before God; James addresses those who think verbal assent without action constitutes faith. The same verse speaks to both errors because it holds faith and relational fidelity together.

**The EMET renders without commentary in the translation body, preserving the exact Hebrew syntax:** "And he believed in the LORD and he reckoned it to him as righteousness." The subject ambiguity is intentional.

---

### On 15:9-10 — מְשֻׁלֶּשֶׁת / בָּתָר: The Covenant Ceremony

**עֶגְלָה מְשֻׁלֶּשֶׁת** — "a heifer threefold."

The adjective **מְשֻׁלֶּשֶׁת** (meshulleshet, Pual participle of שָׁלַשׁ) means "threefold, three-year-old, set in threes." The three animals (heifer, she-goat, ram) are each described with this term, and the two birds (turtledove, young pigeon) are not so described. The most natural reading is that each of the three large animals is **three years old** — a mature animal at the height of its strength and value, appropriate for a solemn covenant ceremony. The Mishnah (Parah 1:1) confirms that a three-year-old heifer is the prime specimen.

**וַיְבַתֵּר אֹתָם בַּתָּוֶךְ** — "and he divided them in the middle."

The verb **בָּתַר** (batar) means "to cut in two, to divide by cutting." It is the technical term for a specific type of covenant ceremony — the cutting of animals — from which the Hebrew idiom **כָּרַת בְּרִית** ("to cut a covenant") takes its meaning. In the ancient Near East (corroborated by treaties from Mari and elsewhere), covenant-cutting involved the sacrifice of animals whose halves were laid out in two rows, with the contracting parties walking between the halves. The symbolic meaning: "May what happened to these animals happen to me if I break this covenant." The covenant-cutters are *passing under the curse of death* as a condition of the covenant.

**וְאֶת-הַצִּפֹּר לֹא בָתָר** — "but the birds he did not divide."

The birds are not divided. This is consistent with Levitical practice (Lev. 1:17; 5:8): birds were not split in two but had their heads wrung or torn to the side without being fully severed. The detail is precise and may reflect actual ancient ritual convention.

---

### On 15:11 — הָעַיִט: The Birds of Prey

**וַיֵּרֶד הָעַיִט עַל-הַפְּגָרִים** — "And the birds of prey came down upon the carcasses."

**הָעַיִט** (ha-ayit) — "the birds of prey" — is a collective noun referring to carrion birds: eagles, vultures, kites. Their descent upon the carcasses is a narrative detail that (a) confirms the realism of the scene — laid-out animal carcasses would naturally attract scavengers — and (b) may carry symbolic weight. Several interpreters read the birds of prey as a negative omen (representing future oppressors of Abram's descendants) that Abram drives away, signaling divine protection. The EMET translates without allegorizing, but notes the interpretive tradition.

**וַיַּשֵּׁב אֹתָם אַבְרָם** — "and Abram drove them away."

The Hiphil **וַיַּשֵּׁב** (from **שׁוּב**, "to return/turn back") in the causative: "he caused them to turn back/go away." Abram actively guards the covenant preparations from defilement. This is the last action Abram takes before the deep sleep falls in v. 12.

---

### On 15:12 — תַּרְדֵּמָה: The Deep Sleep

**וְתַרְדֵּמָה נָפְלָה עַל-אַבְרָם** — "and a deep sleep fell upon Abram."

**תַּרְדֵּמָה** (tardemah) is a specific and significant word. It denotes a divinely induced stupor or trance-sleep, distinct from ordinary sleep (**שֵׁנָה**, shenah). The word appears only a handful of times in the Hebrew Bible:
- **Genesis 2:21:** "And the LORD God caused a deep sleep to fall upon the man" (before the creation of woman from his side)
- **Genesis 15:12:** here
- **1 Samuel 26:12:** the deep sleep that falls upon Saul's camp (YHWH-sent)
- **Isaiah 29:10:** "the LORD has poured out upon you a spirit of deep sleep" (divine stupor as judgment)
- **Proverbs 19:15:** "Sloth casts into deep sleep"
- **Job 4:13; 33:15:** visions come "in deep sleep"

The word's consistent use as a divinely induced or divinely associated state is important. This is not Abram falling asleep of fatigue; it is YHWH causing Abram to enter an ecstatic, receptive state in which the covenant revelation can be given. The connection to Genesis 2:21 is suggestive: both instances involve a divine action that transforms reality while a human is in this special sleep.

**וְהִנֵּה אֵימָה חֲשֵׁכָה גְדֹלָה נֹפֶלֶת עָלָיו** — "and look — a dread, a great darkness falling upon him."

**אֵימָה** (eimah) = "terror, dread, horror" — the characteristic response to theophany and divine encounter. The word appears in key theophanic contexts: Exod. 15:16 (terror on the nations at the Exodus); Exod. 23:27 ("I will send my terror before you"); Deut. 32:25; Isa. 33:18. The pairing with **חֲשֵׁכָה גְדֹלָה** ("great darkness") creates a compound of overwhelming experiential weight: Abram is in darkness, in dread, in divine sleep — the full weight of the holy falling upon him.

---

### On 15:13 — The 400-Year Prophecy

**יָדֹעַ תֵּדַע** — "Knowing you shall know."

The **infinitive absolute + finite verb** (yadoa' teda') is the Hebrew idiom for absolute certainty and emphasis: "you shall surely/certainly know." This is the same emphatic structure as **מוֹת תָּמוּת** ("dying you shall die," "you shall surely die") in Gen. 2:17. The grammar signals that what follows is a certain and solemn declaration.

**אַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה** — "four hundred years."

The prophecy of four hundred years of sojourning and slavery (fulfilled in Exodus) is given here in its round number. The more specific figure **430 years** appears in Exodus 12:40-41 for the period "of the sojourning of the children of Israel who dwelt in Egypt." Paul in Galatians 3:17 uses 430 years for the period from the Abrahamic promise to the giving of the law at Sinai. The relationship between 400 and 430 has generated extensive discussion; the round figure of 400 here in the prophecy and the more precise 430 in Exodus likely reflect different starting points for the calculation (some beginning with Abram himself, some with Jacob's descent into Egypt).

---

### On 15:16 — לֹא-שָׁלֵם עֲוֹן הָאֱמֹרִי עַד-הֵנָּה: The Iniquity of the Amorites

**כִּי לֹא-שָׁלֵם עֲוֹן הָאֱמֹרִי עַד-הֵנָּה** — "for the iniquity of the Amorites is not complete until now."

This is one of the most theologically remarkable statements in the entire Abrahamic narrative. God delays the giving of the land to Abram's descendants not merely for practical reasons (they are not yet ready) but for a moral reason: the current inhabitants of the land — represented here by the Amorites, as the dominant people-group — have not yet reached the measure of iniquity that would warrant divine judgment and displacement. The land transfer is scheduled to occur when the Amorites have "filled up" their iniquity.

**עֲוֹן** (avon) = "iniquity, guilt, the weight of wrongdoing." The word carries both the objective fact of wrongdoing and the subjective weight of guilt that accumulates and demands response.

**שָׁלֵם** (shalem) = "to be complete, whole, finished, paid." The Qal perfect "has not been completed" — the iniquity has not yet reached the point of requiring judgment. The moral calculus is explicit: God is giving the Amorites time (four generations from 15:16, compared with four hundred years from 15:13 — a generational calculation embedded alongside the chronological one). The displacement of the Canaanites in Joshua is thus framed not as ethnic favoritism but as delayed moral judgment that has finally ripened.

This verse is one of the Bible's clearest statements about **divine patience and moral accounting**: God does not execute judgment prematurely. The Amorites are being given time. When the time is complete, the promise to Abram's descendants will be fulfilled — and the moral ledger of the Amorites will be full. This framing transforms the Conquest narrative from a land-grab into an act of deferred cosmic justice.

---

### On 15:17 — תַּנּוּר עָשָׁן וְלַפִּיד אֵשׁ: The Smoking Fire Pot and the Flaming Torch (Theophany)

**וְהִנֵּה תַנּוּר עָשָׁן וְלַפִּיד אֵשׁ אֲשֶׁר עָבַר בֵּין הַגְּזָרִים הָאֵלֶּה** — "and look — a smoking fire pot and a flaming torch that passed between these pieces."

This is the theophanic climax of Genesis 15, and one of the most vivid theophanies in the entire Pentateuch.

**תַּנּוּר עָשָׁן (tannur ashan, "smoking fire pot"):** A **תַּנּוּר** (tannur) is a clay oven or furnace — the kind used for baking bread in the ancient Near East. It is a domestic, earthly object, but here filled with divine smoke. The word appears in theophanic contexts: Exod. 19:18 compares Sinai to a furnace: "the smoke of it went up like the smoke of a kiln [כְּבָשַׁן הָכִּבְשָׁן]." The smoking furnace here carries the same association: smoke = divine presence, the kabod/glory manifesting in visible smoke (Exod. 19:18; Isa. 6:4; Rev. 15:8).

**לַפִּיד אֵשׁ (lappid esh, "flaming torch"):** A **לַפִּיד** (lappid) is a torch, a fire-brand. The association of YHWH with fire is pervasive throughout the theophanic tradition: the burning bush (Exod. 3:2), the pillar of fire (Exod. 13:21-22), the fire on Sinai (Exod. 19:18; 24:17), "our God is a consuming fire" (Deut. 4:24; Heb. 12:29). The lappid esh here anticipates the pillar of fire that will lead Israel through the wilderness — the same fiery presence of YHWH.

**אֲשֶׁר עָבַר בֵּין הַגְּזָרִים (asher avar bein ha-gezarim, "that passed between the pieces"):**

Here is the theological heart of the covenant ceremony. In normal covenant-cutting, *both* parties walked between the pieces, each swearing: "Let what happened to these animals happen to me if I break this covenant." The EMET renders the crucial observation: only YHWH passes between the pieces. Abram is in the tardemah, the deep sleep, unconscious. God walks through the cut covenant ceremony *alone*.

This means: **YHWH takes the covenant oath unilaterally.** He places himself under the self-curse of the covenant — "if these promises are broken, may I be cut in two like these animals" — without requiring Abram to do the same. This is the theological signature of the Abrahamic covenant as distinct from the Mosaic covenant: the Abrahamic covenant is **unconditional, unilateral, and self-imprecatory on God's part**. Its fulfillment depends not on Abram's faithfulness but on YHWH's. The New Testament (Gal. 3:15-18; Heb. 6:13-18) will develop this point: since no one was greater to swear by, YHWH swore by himself, and since God cannot lie, the covenant is absolutely certain.

The **LXX** renders the theophany with full vividness: **κάμινος καπνιζομένη καὶ λαμπάδες πυρός** — "a smoking furnace and torches of fire." The plural **λαμπάδες** (lampades, "torches") is notable — the LXX may read **לַפִּיד** as a collective or may have a slightly different text, but the difference is minor. The image is the same.

---

### On 15:18 — כָּרַת בְּרִית: "Cutting a Covenant"

**כָּרַת יְהוָה אֶת-אַבְרָם בְּרִית** — "the LORD cut with Abram a covenant."

The verb **כָּרַת** (karat) literally means "to cut." The idiom **כָּרַת בְּרִית** ("to cut a covenant") reflects the covenant ceremony just described: the covenant is "cut" because animals are cut in the ratification ceremony. This idiom appears throughout the Hebrew Bible for the making of covenants (with people: Gen. 21:27; 26:28; 31:44; with YHWH: Exod. 24:8; Deut. 29:12; Josh. 24:25; and hundreds more). The EMET preserves the literal "cut a covenant" rather than the conventional "made a covenant."

**מִנְּהַר מִצְרַיִם עַד-הַנָּהָר הַגָּדֹל נְהַר-פְּרָת** — "from the river of Egypt to the great river, the river Euphrates."

The land boundaries given here describe the maximum extent of the promised territory — from the Egyptian frontier (the Wadi el-Arish, or perhaps the Nile itself; **נְהַר מִצְרַיִם** is more likely the Wadi el-Arish than the Nile proper, since the Nile is typically called **יְאֹר**, ye'or) to the Euphrates. This represents the full geographic scope of the land of Canaan and its surrounding regions. Historically, this extent was approximately achieved under Solomon (1 Kings 4:21, 24: "Solomon ruled over all the kingdoms from the Euphrates to the land of the Philistines and to the border of Egypt"). The promise is not a modest local land grant but a massive territorial claim that encompasses the heartland of the ancient Near East.

---

### On 15:19-21 — The Ten Nations

**אֶת-הַקֵּינִי וְאֶת-הַקְּנִזִּי...** — "the Kenite and the Kenizzite..."

The list of ten nations whose territory constitutes the promised land is the fullest version of the peoples-list in the Pentateuch. The standard list of "seven nations" (Deut. 7:1; Acts 13:19) names: Hittites, Girgashites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, Jebusites. The present list in Genesis 15 adds three additional peoples (Kenites, Kenizzites, Kadmonites) not found in the seven-nations lists. This longer version of the peoples-list is specific to the Abrahamic covenant and may reflect an older, more expansive geographic understanding of the promised territory.

**The ten peoples:**
1. **הַקֵּינִי (Kenite):** The Kenites appear later as a people associated with Midian (Judg. 1:16; 4:11, 17; 5:24); Moses' father-in-law Jethro/Hobab is a Kenite. They were nomadic metalworkers (the name may relate to **קַיִן**, Cain, or to the root for "smith/metalworker").
2. **הַקְּנִזִּי (Kenizzite):** Caleb the son of Jephunneh was a Kenizzite (Num. 32:12; Josh. 14:6) — a non-Israelite clan absorbed into Judah. Othniel, first judge, was also from this family.
3. **הַקַּדְמֹנִי (Kadmonite):** "The eastern ones" — perhaps a general term for peoples of the Transjordanian east, or a specific clan. They appear only here.
4. **הַחִתִּי (Hittite):** The Hittites in Canaan (distinct from the great Hittite empire of Anatolia, though related); Ephron from whom Abram buys the Machpelah cave is a Hittite (Gen. 23).
5. **הַפְּרִזִּי (Perizzite):** An indigenous Canaanite people, often listed alongside the Canaanites; their name may mean "villagers" (from **פְּרָזִי**, rural/unwalled settlement).
6. **הָרְפָאִים (Rephaim):** A class of ancient, giant inhabitants of Canaan (Deut. 2:10-11; 3:11-13); associated with King Og of Bashan. Their name may relate to the root **רְפָאִים** in the sense of "shades/spirits of the dead" in later Ugaritic and Hebrew usage, suggesting an ancient and semi-mythological status.
7. **הָאֱמֹרִי (Amorite):** The dominant people-group of Canaan and Transjordan in the patriarchal period (as in Gen. 14-15); the term is sometimes used broadly for all Canaanites.
8. **הַכְּנַעֲנִי (Canaanite):** The eponymous people of the land of Canaan.
9. **הַגִּרְגָּשִׁי (Girgashite):** A Canaanite clan; attested in Ugaritic texts as **grgš**.
10. **הַיְבוּסִי (Jebusite):** The inhabitants of Jerusalem (then called Jebus) before David's conquest (2 Sam. 5:6-9). Araunah/Ornan the Jebusite owned the threshing floor on Mount Moriah where the Temple would be built (2 Sam. 24:16-24).

The **ten nations** may constitute a symbolic fullness (ten being a number of completeness in biblical numerology — cf. the ten commandments, the ten plagues) rather than a precise ethnographic census.

---

## LXX Comparison Summary for Genesis 15

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Codex Vaticanus) | Notes |
|-------|---------------------|----------------------|-------|
| 15:1 | אָנֹכִי מָגֵן לָךְ שְׂכָרְךָ הַרְבֵּה מְאֹד | ἐγώ εἰμι ὑπερασπίζω σου· ὁ μισθός σου πολὺς ἔσται σφόδρα | LXX reads two clauses: "I am shielding you; your reward will be very great." Confirms Reading 1 of the crux; EMET follows MT's paratactic structure |
| 15:2 | בֶּן-מֶשֶׁק בֵּיתִי הוּא דַּמֶּשֶׂק אֱלִיעֶזֶר | ὁ δὲ υἱὸς Μασεκ τῆς οἰκογενοῦς μου, οὗτος Δαμασκὸς Ἐλιέζερ | LXX treats מֶשֶׁק as a proper name (Masek); likely secondary interpretation. MT reads as a common noun construct |
| 15:5 | הַבֶּט-נָא הַשָּׁמַיְמָה | ἀνάβλεψον δὴ εἰς τὸν οὐρανόν | LXX: "Look up now toward the heaven." Exact equivalence |
| 15:6 | וְהֶאֱמִן בַּיהוָה | ἐπίστευσεν Αβραμ τῷ θεῷ | LXX: "Abram trusted God." The dative (τῷ θεῷ) vs. MT's bet preposition (בַּ). Paul quotes LXX form verbatim in Rom. 4:3 |
| 15:6 | וַיַּחְשְׁבֶהָ לוֹ צְדָקָה | καὶ ἐλογίσθη αὐτῷ εἰς δικαιοσύνην | LXX uses passive ἐλογίσθη ("it was reckoned/counted"), which removes the subject ambiguity of the MT — the passive implies God as implied agent. MT preserves ambiguity |
| 15:9 | מְשֻׁלֶּשֶׁת | τριετίζουσαν / τριετίζοντα | LXX: "three-year-old" (τριετίζω = "to be three years old"). Confirms age-reading of the Hebrew adjective |
| 15:12 | וְתַרְדֵּמָה נָפְלָה עַל-אַבְרָם | ἔκστασις δὲ ἐπέπεσεν τῷ Αβραμ | LXX: "and an ecstasy/trance fell upon Abram." ἔκστασις (ekstasis, "ecstasy, trance, standing outside oneself") for תַּרְדֵּמָה (tardemah). LXX heightens the ecstatic/visionary character; MT is more neutral "deep sleep" |
| 15:12 | אֵימָה חֲשֵׁכָה גְדֹלָה | φόβος σκοτεινός μέγας | LXX: "a great dark fear." φόβος = fear/dread. Confirms MT's pairing of terror and darkness |
| 15:17 | תַּנּוּר עָשָׁן וְלַפִּיד אֵשׁ | κάμινος καπνιζομένη καὶ λαμπάδες πυρός | LXX: "a smoking furnace and torches of fire." Notable: LXX uses plural λαμπάδες ("torches") where MT has singular לַפִּיד. Minor variant; does not affect theology |
| 15:18 | כָּרַת יְהוָה אֶת-אַבְרָם בְּרִית | διέθετο κύριος τῷ Αβραμ διαθήκην | LXX uses διατίθημι ("to set/arrange/make a covenant," διαθήκη = covenant/testament) rather than a verb meaning "to cut." Greek lacks the cutting idiom; LXX loses the "cut" imagery that underlies the Hebrew idiom. The LXX word διαθήκη (diatheke) becomes the standard NT word for "covenant/testament" |
| 15:21 | גִּרְגָּשִׁי | Γεργεσαίων | LXX: Gergesaioi. The LXX form gives us the "Gadarenes/Gergesenes" of the NT Gospels (Matt. 8:28) |

---

## Dead Sea Scrolls, Samaritan Pentateuch, and Versional Notes

**Dead Sea Scrolls:** The **4QGen-a** fragments (4Q1) may preserve portions of Genesis 15, though the coverage is not complete. Where attested, they are consistent with the Masoretic text. No variant affecting the major cruxes of Genesis 15 has been identified in the published DSS transcriptions (DJD XII, Ulrich et al.). The Qumran text of Genesis generally confirms the MT tradition for the patriarchal narratives.

**Samaritan Pentateuch:** The SP agrees with the MT throughout Genesis 15 without significant substantive variants. Minor orthographic differences only.

**Genesis 15:6 in the New Testament — Full Citation Register:**
- **Romans 4:3:** "For what does the Scripture say? 'Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him as righteousness.'" (Paul, quoting LXX: ἐπίστευσεν δὲ Ἀβραὰμ τῷ θεῷ, καὶ ἐλογίσθη αὐτῷ εἰς δικαιοσύνην)
- **Romans 4:9, 22; Galatians 3:6:** Further citations and applications of the same verse.
- **James 2:23:** "And the Scripture was fulfilled that says, 'Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him as righteousness'" — used to demonstrate that faith and works are inseparable in the Abrahamic model.
- Genesis 15:6 is thus the most-quoted single verse from Genesis in the NT and arguably in all of Christian theology.

**Hebrews 6:13-18 on the Abrahamic covenant:** The Letter to the Hebrews explicitly addresses the unilateral divine oath of Genesis 15: "For when God made a promise to Abraham, since he had no one greater by whom to swear, he swore by himself... So when God desired to show more convincingly to the heirs of the promise the unchangeable character of his purpose, he guaranteed it with an oath, so that by two unchangeable things, in which it is impossible for God to lie, we who have fled for refuge might have strong encouragement to hold fast to the hope set before us." The two unchangeable things are the promise and the oath — both given by God in the covenant of Genesis 15.

**Galatians 3:15-18 on covenant priority:** Paul argues that the Abrahamic covenant (ratified in Gen. 15) is a διαθήκη (testament/covenant) that was "ratified by God" and cannot be "annulled" or "added to" — the Mosaic law (430 years later) does not override or modify it. The unconditional character of the Genesis 15 covenant — in which only God passes between the pieces — is foundational to this argument.

**Targum Onkelos:** Renders Genesis 15 closely following the MT. At 15:1, Onkelos renders **מָגֵן** as *magena* (Aramaic "shield"). At 15:6, Onkelos renders **צְדָקָה** as *zekhu* (merit/righteousness) — the standard Aramaic equivalent. The subject ambiguity of **וַיַּחְשְׁבֶהָ** is rendered with God as the implied subject in Onkelos: "and it was accounted to him as merit."

**Targum Pseudo-Jonathan** at 15:12 expands dramatically, interpreting the **אֵימָה** (dread) as a prophetic vision of the four world empires that will oppress Israel (Babylon, Persia/Media, Greece, Rome/Edom). This is a classic example of pesher-style interpretation where the deep sleep becomes an apocalyptic vision. The EMET notes this tradition as illustrative of ancient interpretive expansion; it does not affect the translation.

**Philo of Alexandria (Quis Rerum Divinarum Heres, §§ 249-316):** Philo provides an extended allegorical interpretation of the covenant ceremony, treating the divided animals as symbols of the cosmos divided into its constituent elements. He understands Abraham's driving away of the birds as the philosophical mind rejecting false opinions. The EMET notes Philo's interpretation as a witness to ancient exegesis; the translation follows the plain sense.
